bibliographic_data.title: A Color-Magnitude Diagram for a Globular Cluster In the Giant Elliptical Galaxy NGC 5128
bibliographic_data.abstract: The Hubble Space Telescope has been used to obtain WFPC2 (V, I) photometry for a large sample of stars in the outer halo of the giant elliptical NGC 5128, at a distance 4 Mpc beyond the Local Group. The globular cluster N5128-C44, at the center of the Planetary Camera field, is well enough resolved to permit the construction of a color-magnitude diagram (CMD) for it which covers the brightest two magnitudes of the giant branch. The CMD is consistent with that of a normal old, moderately low-metallicity ([Fe/H] ≃−1.3) globular cluster, distinctly more metal-poor than most of the field halo stars at the same projected location (which average [Fe/H] ∼−0.5). This is the most distant globular cluster in which direct color-magnitude photometry has been achieved to date, and the first one belonging to a giant E galaxy.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents the most distant globular cluster in which direct color-magnitude photometry has been achieved, and provides evidence that its metallicity is consistent with a normal old, moderately low-metallicity globular cluster.
core_content.key_methodology: WFPC2 (V, I) photometry was used to obtain CMD for N5128-C44 in the outer halo of NGC 5128.
outcomes: The CMD is consistent with a normal old, moderately low-metallicity globular cluster
N5128-C44 is more metal-poor than most field halo stars at the same projected location
core_content.full_text_markdown:
The paper discusses the use of Hubble Space Telescope's WFPC2 to obtain photometric data for a globular cluster in NGC 5128, providing direct color-magnitude information. The cluster N5128-C44 is analyzed, and its CMD suggests it has properties consistent with an old, low-metallicity cluster. This work represents the most distant such study achieved to date.

The observations were conducted on two visits in 1997, using WFPC2 filters F606W (V) and F814W (I). The data reduction process is detailed elsewhere, but here the authors present results for the PC1 field. The CMD covers about 2.5 magnitudes of the old red giant branch and includes over 10,000 stars.

The metallicity of N5128-C44 was estimated to be [Fe/H] = −1.4 ± 0.25, similar to Milky Way halo clusters and field stars. The cluster's radial velocity is also consistent with the systemic mean of its host galaxy.
